residence.notes: Lived in Mexico working on Imperial Mexican Railway under Maximilian. After 1867, family returned to Washington DC.

parents.mother_research_notes: Harriet Randolph Hackley (I00270) — Andrew's second wife, mother of all 11 children including Charles.

sources.short: The Evening Star (Washington DC) Oct 25, 1899 page 8

notable.notable_blurb: Confederate engineer who built the Erie Canal expansion

military_service.notes: Officer in the Confederate Army during the Civil War. Specific unit and rank not yet researched. Source: Evening Star (Washington DC) Oct 25, 1899 page 8.

career.notes: Worked on the Erie Canal enlargement project.

career.role: Civil Engineer — Washington DC Water Works

career.notes: Held a "high official position" per the Daily Critic. Family lived in Mexico City and were honored guests at Maximilian and Carlotta's court via Theodosia's uncle and aunt who served in the imperial household.

narrative_blocks.header: Confederate engineer turned imperial railway builder
narrative_blocks.body: Charles served as a Confederate engineer in the Civil War, then took a senior post on the Imperial Mexican Railway under Emperor Maximilian. His wife's uncle and aunt were members of the imperial household, and the family were honored guests at Maximilian and Carlotta's court.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent his wife and children out under armed
narrative_blocks.body: When the French army withdrew from Mexico in 1867 and Maximilian was left to his death, Charles arranged for his wife and five children to flee Mexico City under armed escort to a place of safety. He himself died shortly afterward — his widow returned to Washington with no fortune and took a clerk's job at the Patent Office to support the children.

residence.notes: Lived in Mexico working on Imperial Mexican Railway under Maximilian. After 1867, family returned to Washington DC.

parents.mother_research_notes: Harriet Randolph Hackley (I00270) — Andrew's second wife, mother of all 11 children including Charles.

sources.short: The Evening Star (Washington DC) Oct 25, 1899 page 8

notable.notable_blurb: Confederate engineer who built the Erie Canal expansion

military_service.notes: Officer in the Confederate Army during the Civil War. Specific unit and rank not yet researched. Source: Evening Star (Washington DC) Oct 25, 1899 page 8.

career.notes: Worked on the Erie Canal enlargement project.

career.role: Civil Engineer — Washington DC Water Works

career.notes: Held a "high official position" per the Daily Critic. Family lived in Mexico City and were honored guests at Maximilian and Carlotta's court via Theodosia's uncle and aunt who served in the imperial household.

narrative_blocks.header: Confederate engineer turned imperial railway builder
narrative_blocks.body: Charles served as a Confederate engineer in the Civil War, then took a senior post on the Imperial Mexican Railway under Emperor Maximilian. His wife's uncle and aunt were members of the imperial household, and the family were honored guests at Maximilian and Carlotta's court.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent his wife and children out under armed
narrative_blocks.body: When the French army withdrew from Mexico in 1867 and Maximilian was left to his death, Charles arranged for his wife and five children to flee Mexico City under armed escort to a place of safety. He himself died shortly afterward — his widow returned to Washington with no fortune and took a clerk's job at the Patent Office to support the children.

sources.short: United Register of Civil, Military and Naval Service 1875-1899

career.organization: U.S. Patent Office, Department of the Interior
career.notes: Worked at the Patent Office for 30 years — 1869 to 1899. Per Geoffrey D. Austrian's 'Herman Hollerith: Forgotten Giant of Information Processing' (Columbia University Press, 1982): 'Theodosia was one of the first female career employees at the Patent Office, and her income kept her family, and that of her son-in-law, financially solvent during Hollerith's down periods.' She left the Patent Office in 1899, the same year she married Thomas Hambleton.

narrative_blocks.header: The pretty and brilliant Miss Theo. Barnard
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia Barnard was remembered in 1870s Georgetown as 'the pretty and brilliant Miss Theo. Barnard,' the Daily Critic noted on her son's Annapolis admission. Her family's connection to Maximilian's imperial household took her to Mexico City, and her armed flight from the capital after the empire's fall became part of family lore.

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty years a Patent Office clerk
narrative_blocks.body: After Charles died in 1867 leaving her with five children and no fortune, Theodosia took a clerk position at the U.S. Patent Office where she remained from 1869 to 1899 — one of the first women career federal employees. Per Geoffrey Austrian's 1982 Hollerith biography, her income also helped sustain her son-in-law Herman Hollerith's family during his early lean years.

burial.plot_notes: Oak Hill Cemetery, Washington DC. Funeral held July 29, 1889 at the chapel.

notable.notable_blurb: Youngest cadet engineer ever admitted to Annapolis (1875)

education.notes: Admitted Sept 15, 1875 at age 16 — youngest cadet engineer ever admitted at the time. Passed 18th of 140 competitors. Per the Daily Critic: had attended American schools only the past few years, having previously lived in Mexico.

career.notes: Graduated USNA 1881; appointed Assistant Engineer. Assigned to USS Atlanta early 1888.

military_service.notes: Naval Academy 1875-1881. Assistant Engineer. Last assignment: USS Atlanta. Died July 25, 1889 in the bathroom of the USS Atlanta in Brooklyn Navy Yard, by self-inflicted gunshot wound to the right temple with his service revolver. Coroner's inquest verdict: "death by a pistol shot wound inflicted by himself during temporary aberration of mind."

narrative_blocks.header: The youngest cadet engineer ever admitted
narrative_blocks.body: Charlie Talcott entered the Naval Academy in September 1875 at age sixteen, having spent most of his childhood in Mexico during his father's railway work. The Daily Critic noted he was 'the youngest cadet engineer ever admitted,' passing eighteenth of one hundred and forty competitors despite having attended American schools only the past few years.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed himself in the warship's bathroom
narrative_blocks.body: On July 25, 1889 the officers of the USS Atlanta heard what they took for a friction match and noticed nothing until a sailor heard groans from the bathroom. Lt. Cmdr. Cowden forced the door and found Asst.

burial.plot_notes: Oak Hill Cemetery DC. Davidson Lot 9 East — unmarked burial.

sources.short: Harry Talcott obituary (per compiler note: assistant chief engineer of B&O Railroad)

notable.notable_blurb: Ran the railroad through Alaska’s White Pass

career.organization: White Pass and Yukon Route Railroad
career.notes: First railroad in Alaska — built across White Pass during/after the Klondike Gold Rush. Sam's compiler note: 'superintendent of first railroad in Alaska around 1900, going through famous White pass.'
Per his obituary: 'assistant chief engineer of B&O railroad.' Specific years not documented.

narrative_blocks.header: Ran the first railroad through Alaska's White Pass
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1900, Harry Talcott was superintendent of Alaska's first railroad — the White Pass and Yukon Route — built across the brutal pass that had killed Klondike Gold Rush stampeders three years earlier. He returned to civilian rail engineering as assistant chief engineer of the B&O Railroad, and was buried in an unmarked grave at Oak Hill Cemetery in Washington.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in wartime Richmond, raised in Mexico
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Confederate Richmond in 1861 and spent childhood years in Mexico during his father’s Imperial Mexican Railway service before the family settled in Washington. He lies in an unmarked grave on the family’s Oak Hill lot.

birth.state: Veracruz de Ignacio de la Llave

burial.plot_notes: Oak Hill Cemetery, Washington DC. Amphitheater Lot 654 East — Hollerith family plot, alongside husband Herman Hollerith Sr.

notable.notable_blurb: Born in Veracruz during the Imperial Mexican Railway

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Veracruz during the railway work
narrative_blocks.body: Lucia Beverly Talcott was born in Veracruz on December 3, 1865, while her father Charles served as senior engineer on the Imperial Mexican Railway under Emperor Maximilian. Her grandfather Andrew Talcott was supervising the same project; two years later the empire fell and the family fled Mexico City under armed escort.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the inventor of the punch card
narrative_blocks.body: On September 15, 1890, Lucia married Herman Hollerith, the German-American statistician whose punched-card tabulating machine had just revolutionized the 1890 U.S. Census. They had six children, and Hollerith's company would merge in 1924 to form what became IBM — completing a circle her grandson Edmund T. Barclay would close with a 37-year IBM career.

residence.notes: Family home in Georgetown, Washington DC. Summered at 'Brighton' estate in Mathews County, Virginia.

burial.plot_notes: Oak Hill Cemetery, Washington DC. Amphitheater Lot 654 East. GPS: 38.9123993, -77.0556488. Findagrave Memorial ID 14503488.

residence.notes: Lived in Georgetown DC, with home on 29th Street and a business building at 31st Street and the Chesapeake and Ohio Canal — today there is a commemorative plaque installed by IBM at the business location.

parents.father_research_notes: Georg Hollerith — German immigrant, "Forty-eighter" (German revolutionary refugee from the failed revolutions of 1848); school teacher and Lutheran minister from Großfischlingen, Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother's identity not yet documented; also of German origin.

sources.short: USPTO — Count Me In (Journeys of Innovation)
Cortada, James — Before the Computer (historical commentary)

notable.notable_blurb: Invented IBM's first punch-card tabulating machine

career.notes: First job after Columbia. Hired at the invitation of W.P. Trowbridge, his former Columbia professor. Witnessed firsthand the slow processing of the 1880 Census (results not fully tabulated until 1887). Met John Shaw Billings, director of the Division of Vital Statistics, who suggested mechanical methods could help.
Taught mechanical engineering at MIT and conducted his first experiments with punched cards during this period.

Filed patents starting Sept 23, 1884. U.S. Patent 395,782 issued Jan 8, 1889 (along with companion patents 395,781 and 395,783). The system used punched cards to record data, with electrical circuits through perforations operating mechanical counters via electromagnets.

Initially did business under his own name. Provided tabulators to the U.S. Census Bureau for the 1890 Census. The 1890 Census was processed in 6 years vs. 8 years for the 1880 Census, despite a larger population.

Founded 1896. Major census bureaus around the world (England & Wales, Italy, Germany, Russia, Austria, Canada, France, Norway, Puerto Rico, Cuba, Philippines) leased his equipment. Major insurance companies were also customers.

His Tabulating Machine Company merged with three other companies in 1911 to form CTR. Under Thomas J. Watson's presidency, CTR was renamed International Business Machines Corporation (IBM) in 1924. By 1933 the Tabulating Machine Company name had disappeared as the subsidiary was fully subsumed by IBM.

narrative_blocks.header: Invented the punch card that became IBM
narrative_blocks.body: His three patents on the 'Art of Compiling Statistics' were issued January 8, 1889. The system was first deployed for the 1890 U.S. Census — processed in 6 years versus 8 for the smaller 1880 count — and his Tabulating Machine Company was renamed IBM in 1924.

narrative_blocks.header: The conversation that changed data processing
narrative_blocks.body: While at the U.S. Census Bureau in 1880 he met John Shaw Billings, who told him 'there ought to be some mechanical way of doing this job.' Historian James Cortada later called the exchange a singular moment in the history of data processing.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of a Forty-eighter German revolutionary refugee
narrative_blocks.body: His father Georg Hollerith was a Forty-eighter — a refugee of the failed 1848 German revolutions — who emigrated from the Rhineland-Palatinate and worked as a Lutheran minister and schoolteacher. The son's tabulating empire grew from this immigrant educator's household.

narrative_blocks.header: Two great-grandsons lead Episcopal cathedral and diocese
narrative_blocks.body: His great-grandson Randolph Marshall Hollerith serves as Dean of Washington National Cathedral, while another great-grandson Rt. Rev. Herman Hollerith IV is Episcopal Bishop of the Diocese of Southern Virginia. Two heirs of his computing empire turned to ministry.

cross_connections.display_label: His grandnephew-by-marriage spent 37 years as an IBM executive
cross_connections.description: Herman Hollerith's wife Lucia Talcott (T01872) had a brother Edmund Meyers Talcott (T01873). Edmund's grandson — also Edmund (LT Edmund T. Barclay, TD0080) — spent 37 years as an IBM executive: a direct family-line return to the company Herman had founded in 1896.

cross_connections.display_label: His great-grandson became dean of Washington National Cathedral
cross_connections.description: Herman Hollerith's great-grandson Randolph Marshall Hollerith was named the 11th Dean of Washington National Cathedral in 2016, and has led State Funerals for Presidents Bush and Carter and funeral rites for Senators McCain, Dole, and Warner.

cross_connections.display_label: His great-grandson 'Holly' became Episcopal Bishop of Southern Virginia
cross_connections.description: Herman Hollerith's great-grandson and namesake Herman Hollerith IV (consecrated 2009) served as the 10th Bishop of the Episcopal Diocese of Southern Virginia from 2009 to 2018.

education.notes: Joined as faculty teaching mechanical engineering. Conducted his first experiments with punched cards while at MIT.

PhD awarded based on his development of the tabulating system. Doctoral thesis: 'An Electric Tabulating System' (1888-1889 volume of The School of Mines Quarterly), reprinted in Brian Randell's 1982 'The Origins of Digital Computers, Selected Papers.'

awards.name: Elliott Cresson Medal
awards.notes: Awarded for the punched-card tabulating machine.

burial.plot_notes: Oak Hill Cemetery, Amphitheater Lot 654 East — Hollerith family plot.

career.notes: Per WikiTree biographical entry.

notable.notable_blurb: last of the five Talcott siblings to die

narrative_blocks.header: The last living link to the Maximilian flight
narrative_blocks.body: Edmund was eleven months old when his family fled Mexico City under armed escort in 1867, and he became the last of the five Talcott siblings to die — outliving his Hollerith sister Lucia by sixteen years and his suicide brother Charlie by seventy-one. He died at his home on 38th Street NW in Washington in January 1960, age 93.

narrative_blocks.header: Three spellings of his middle name
narrative_blocks.body: The Talcott Genealogy wrote “Myers,” his obituary agreed, and his gravestone says “Meyers” — the tree follows the stone. He died at home in Washington at ninety-three and lies at Oak Hill beside Cordelia, his wife of six decades.

burial.plot_notes: Hollywood Cemetery, Richmond VA presumed (same as wife and son Charles Sr); not yet confirmed.

parents.father_research_notes: George Talcott Sr. (1755-1813), farmer at the Glastenbury homestead descended from Benjamin Talcott. Served briefly in the Revolutionary War.
parents.mother_research_notes: Abigail Goodrich (1767-1854), daughter of John and Abigail Deming Goodrich, m. George Sr. Feb 9, 1786 as his second wife after Vienna Bradford's death. Andrew was her youngest child. I-prefix entry pending.

sources.short: Findagrave (Lucia Hollerith biographical material citing Andrew Talcott 1797-1883)

narrative_blocks.header: From West Point to Maximilian's imperial railway
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated USMA 1818 second in his class, oversaw construction of Forts Adams, Monroe, Calhoun, and Hamilton, then surveyed the Ohio-Michigan border alongside Lt. Robert E. Lee. Late in life he engineered the steepest railway built up to that time — Veracruz to Mexico City for Maximilian's regime.

narrative_blocks.header: Stayed neutral, jailed as a Confederate spy anyway
narrative_blocks.body: Though his son served on Lee's staff and Lee called his wife 'the Beautiful Talcott,' he refused to fight for either side and went back to Mexico. Returning briefly to New York for railway supplies in March 1863, he was arrested as a CSA spy and held at Forts Lafayette and Warren before Gen. Dix released him.

narrative_blocks.header: The Horrebow-Talcott latitude method bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: In 1833 he extended a method by Danish astronomer Peder Horrebow for fixing latitude by observing zenith distances of stars on opposite sides of the zenith. The technique came to be called the Horrebow-Talcott method and was used by geodesists into the 20th century.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter married IBM founder Herman Hollerith
narrative_blocks.body: His granddaughter Lucia Beverly Talcott married Herman Hollerith on September 15, 1890. Hollerith's tabulating company became Computing-Tabulating-Recording in 1911 and was renamed IBM in 1924 — Andrew's descendants thus woven into the founding family of computing.

narrative_blocks.header: Lee called his wife 'the Beautiful Talcott'
narrative_blocks.body: He was Robert E. Lee's superior and friend at Hampton Roads from the 1820s, surveying the Ohio-Michigan border with him in 1835. Lee affectionately called his wife Harriet 'the Beautiful Talcott' for half a century.

narrative_blocks.header: His name is on a latitude method
narrative_blocks.body: Extending Peder Horrebow’s technique, Talcott’s 1833 refinement for fixing latitude from paired zenith stars became the Horrebow-Talcott Method, which still bears his name. The American Philosophical Society elected him in 1838.

narrative_blocks.header: Robert E. Lee worked under him
narrative_blocks.body: As superintending engineer at Forts Calhoun and Monroe on Hampton Roads he was superior and friend to the young Lee, surveying the Ohio-Michigan border with him in 1835 before resigning his captaincy for civil practice.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father-in-law whose Florida claim he sued to defend

education.institution: United States Military Academy at West Point
education.degree: Graduated July 24, 1818 — No. 2 in his class (No. 1 was Gen. Richard Delafield)
education.notes: Cadet from March 14, 1815 to July 24, 1818. Classmates included Gen. Harvey Brown, Major Ringold, and Lt. Col. Hartman Bache.

career.notes: First post after Army resignation. Specifically western division of the line.
Worked alongside young A. B. (Andrew) Gray.
Member of the commission for exploration and survey of the north-east boundary.
Examining Portsmouth and Pensacola navy yards and projecting stone and floating docks.
Jan 11, 1848 - July 31, 1855. Later appointed general manager. Note: was considered for Superintendent of the Coast Survey 1843 but the post went to Alexander Bache.

career.organization: Ohio and Mississippi Railroad (Cincinnati to St. Louis)
Mexico and Pacific Railway (Vera Cruz - Mexico City - Pacific)
career.notes: From Dec 1, 1857. Engaged by A. Escandon (with English financing) — supervised W. W. Finney of the Pony Express. Andrew was assigned the more difficult southern passage (rising 211 ft/mile over 23 miles, with the 380-ft Metlac River iron bridge — at the time the steepest railway ever undertaken). Suspended 1861-1862 when Civil War began (he became Virginia State Engineer Mar 1861 - Dec 1861); resumed under French-reorganized concession late 1860s under Maximilian until Juárez defeated Maximilian 1867.

career.role: Chief Engineer of the State of Virginia
career.organization: Commonwealth of Virginia (NOT Confederate States Army)
career.notes: Appointed March 1861 at request of Robert E. Lee; Colonel and State Engineer of Virginia. Charged with superintendence of river, coast, and harbor defenses of the State (Richmond and James River). Built/rebuilt Fort Boykin (star-shaped) and Fort Huger — both later crippled by ironclad USS Galena. Returned to Mexico when CSA government assumed the work in December 1861.

career.organization: Bon Air, Chesterfield County, Virginia (with son TMR Talcott)
career.notes: After returning from Mexico in 1867, with his son Col. Thomas Mann Randolph Talcott (T01842) invested in development of the Bon Air, VA community south of Richmond.

awards.name: Member, American Philosophical Society
awards.notes: Elected 1838 in recognition of his engineering and surveying contributions, particularly the Horrebow-Talcott Method of latitude determination.

military_service.war: Pre-Civil-War U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (peacetime)
military_service.unit: Corps of Engineers (Brevet 2nd Lt 1818 → 2nd Lt 1818 → 1st Lt 1820 → Capt 1830 → resigned 1836)
military_service.notes: Promoted Brevet 2nd Lt CoE July 24, 1818 (graduation day). Asst Engineer at Fort at Rouse's Point, NY 1818-19. 2nd Lt CoE Aug 14, 1818. Aide-de-camp to Bvt. Brig. Gen. Atkinson + Engineer on expedition establishing forts on upper Missouri and Yellowstone rivers Nov 1, 1820 - April 30, 1821. 1st Lt CoE Oct 1, 1820. Asst Engineer at defenses of Hampton Roads VA 1821-24. Engineer in charge of preliminary operations for Brenton's Point (Fort Adams RI), New Utrecht Point (Fort Hamilton NY) 1824-25; Fort Delaware DE 1825-26; Dismal Swamp Canal 1826-28; Fort Monroe 1828-34; Fort Calhoun 1828-35 (Hampton Roads VA). Brevet Captain Oct 1, 1830 for ten years faithful service. Astronomer for Ohio-Michigan boundary determination Dec 1, 1832 - May 30, 1836 (surveyed alongside then-Lt. Robert E. Lee in spring 1835). Captain CoE Dec 22, 1830. Superintending Engineer for Hudson River improvement NY 1834-36. Resigned September 21, 1836.

narrative_blocks.header: A platonic companion to Robert Lee
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband’s young subordinate at Fort Monroe, Robert E. Lee, became a lifelong family friend — and Harriet, the engineering histories record, his close platonic companion. She bore Andrew twelve children, of whom this tree so far holds two.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children with the Army engineer
narrative_blocks.body: She married Capt. Andrew Talcott in 1832 and bore twelve children, of whom this tree so far holds two; through her the line carries the Randolphs of Tuckahoe and Virginia’s first families. She lies at Hollywood Cemetery among the Richmond dead.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Bradford of the Mayflower
narrative_blocks.body: George Talcott's first wife, Vienna Bradford, was a direct descendant of Governor William Bradford of the Mayflower. She died in 1785 at age 27. George remarried the following year, had six more children, and lived on the family homestead in Glastonbury — land his family had farmed since his grandfather's 1699 deed.

narrative_blocks.header: At the Long Island retreat with his father
narrative_blocks.body: He served in the Revolution alongside his father Col. Elizur and his brothers, present at the army’s night escape from Long Island. A Glastonbury farmer, he held the homestead that came down through his father.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife traced to the Mayflower
narrative_blocks.body: He married Vienna Bradford in 1777 — of the Plymouth Bradford line — and lost her at twenty-seven. His second marriage, to Abigail Goodrich, lasted until his death from “inflammation of the brain” in 1813.

notable.notable_blurb: US Consul at Cadiz; Florida land claimant

narrative_blocks.header: He claimed twelve million Florida acres
narrative_blocks.body: Deeded the tract by the Duke of Alagon in 1819, Jefferson’s former consul at Cadiz asserted ownership of a Florida the size of a small state. The Supreme Court finally extinguished the claim in 1908 — sixty-five years after his death, one of the longest-running land cases in American law.

narrative_blocks.header: A grave marked only R.S.H.
narrative_blocks.body: His large vault-stone in Norfolk’s Cedar Grove Cemetery bears nothing but initials and a year — “R.S.H., 1843” — in an otherwise empty family plot, leaving a modern cemetery researcher to rediscover the consul who once claimed Florida.

cross_connections.display_label: married his daughter Harriet and pressed his Florida claim

narrative_blocks.header: Dolley Madison’s best friend from Tuckahoe
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Tuckahoe plantation to Col. Thomas Mann Randolph, she counted Dolley Madison among her closest friends. Her brother married Martha Jefferson — making her sister-in-law to the President’s daughter — and rose to the governorship of Virginia.

burialCemetery.name: Hollywood Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic Richmond cemetery, burial site of Confederate dead and notable Virginians. Charles Gratiot Talcott Sr. (T01839) buried here.
